mnemonics.title: SHF - Simple Has Fun
mnemonics.content: S-H-F stands for Simplex (one-way), Half-Duplex (two-way but not simultaneous), and Full-Duplex (two-way simultaneously). Remember: Simple Has Fun - like a one-way road, a walkie-talkie conversation, and a telephone call respectively.

mnemonics.title: HUGS RIB - Network Devices
mnemonics.content: HUB, SWITCH, GATEWAY, SERVER, REPEATER, INTERFACE (NIC), BRIDGE - Remember 'HUGS RIB' as devices that connect computers in a network.

mnemonics.title: Routers Route Traffic
mnemonics.content: ROUTER = Routes Outside Using Technical External Routes - connects different networks and finds best path for data.

mnemonics.title: Switch Sends Wisely
mnemonics.content: SWITCH = Sends Where Information To Correct Host - learns MAC addresses and sends data only to intended recipient.

mnemonics.title: Hub Hands Unwanted Broadcasts
mnemonics.content: HUB = Hands Unwanted Broadcasts - broadcasts data to all connected devices, no filtering.

mnemonics.title: Gateway Goes Between Networks
mnemonics.content: GATEWAY = Gateway Access To Extend Networks - connects networks using different protocols.

mnemonics.title: Repeater Regenerates
mnemonics.content: REPEATER = Repeats Electrical Pulses To Extend Reach - amplifies weak signals in network.

acronyms.usage: Hardware component that allows computers to connect to a network. Every device has a unique MAC address on the NIC.
Unique hardware address assigned to network interfaces for communication at the Data Link Layer.
Logical addressing system used by routers to forward packets across networks.
Reliable, connection-oriented protocol ensuring ordered delivery of data.
Faster but unreliable protocol for real-time applications like video streaming.
Network confined to a small geographic area like a building or campus.
Network spanning large geographic areas, often connecting multiple LANs.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a one-way street with cars only moving in one direction, or a TV remote sending signals to the TV (remote → TV only).
Think of a walkie-talkie - you press to talk, release to listen. Only one direction at a time, like a single-lane bridge with traffic controllers.
Picture a telephone conversation where both people can speak and listen simultaneously, or a two-way highway with separate lanes in each direction.
Hub = party line phone where everyone hears every call. Switch = modern phone that rings only the intended person.
Think of a post office sorting mail - routers examine addresses (IP) and send packets to correct destination network.
Draw a pizza: Please (Physical), Do (Data Link), Not (Network), Throw (Transport), Sausage (Session), Pizza (Presentation), Away (Application) - 7 layers from bottom to top.

analogies.analogy: Like a teacher speaking to the entire class through a microphone - everyone hears everything whether it's for them or not.
Like a reception desk at an office - the receptionist knows which employee sits where and forwards calls/messages only to the right person.
Like a city traffic control system or highway interchange - directs vehicles (data packets) to different destinations (networks) via best routes.
Like an airport customs checkpoint connecting international flights to domestic terminals - translates between different 'languages' (protocols).
Like a signal booster on a long mountain road - amplifies a weak radio signal so it can travel farther.
Like connecting two islands with a bridge - joins two separate network segments and manages traffic between them.
Like a home address for your computer - provides the unique MAC address needed for local delivery of packages (data).
Like a flashlight shining in one direction only - it sends light but doesn't receive any.
Like a CB radio or walkie-talkie - you must wait for the other person to finish before you can transmit.
Like a face-to-face conversation - both parties can speak and listen at the same time.
